title: AI and work: an expert assesses how far this revolution still has to run

paragraphs.english: Every week brings fresh claims about AI transforming the workplace. A CEO declares a revolution. A think piece predicts millions of jobs vanishing overnight. The noise is relentless.
paragraphs.pidgin: Every week brings fresh claims about AI transforming di workplace. A CEO declares a revolution. A think piece predicts millions of jobs vanishing overnight. Di noise na relentless.

paragraphs.english: But strip away the hype and there is a simpler question. In developed economies, what has AI actually changed about work so far? The answer turns out to be more interesting, and more uneven, than either side suggests.
paragraphs.pidgin: But strip comot di hype and e dey a simpler question. For develop economies, wetin get AI actually change about work so far? Di answer turns out to be more interesting, and more uneven, than either side suggests.

paragraphs.english: Let’s start with what the evidence supports. AI is delivering genuine productivity gains in specific kinds of knowledge-based and service work. An experimental study found that professionals using ChatGPT for writing tasks took 40% less time to complete them, with an 18% improvement in quality (as evaluated by their colleagues in blind testing).
paragraphs.pidgin: Let’s start wit wetin di evidence supports. AI dey delivere genuine productivity gains for specific kinds of knowledge-based and service work. An experimental study find tok professionals using ChatGPT for writing tasks take 40% less time to complete dem, wit an 18% improvement for quality (as evaluated by dem colleagues for blind testing).

paragraphs.english: And another study of more than 5,000 customer service agents found a 15% increase in issues resolved per hour. An industry experiment involving realistic, complex tasks done with management consultants found they completed the work 25% faster and produced results that were deemed to be 40% higher in quality (again, judged by experts in blind tests). Randomised trials involving nearly 5,000 software developers documented a 26% increase in completed tasks.
paragraphs.pidgin: And another study of more than 5,000 customer service agents find a 15% increase for issues resolved per hour. An industry experiment involving realistic, complex tasks done wit management consultants find dem completed di work 25% faster and produced results dat bin dey deemed to be 40% higher for quality (again, judged by experts for blind tests). Randomised trials involving nearly 5,000 software developers documented a 26% increase for completed tasks.

paragraphs.english: AI has long been discussed as a threat to jobs and livelihoods. But what’s the reality? In this new series, we explore the impact it is already having on different occupations – and how people really feel about their AI assistants.
paragraphs.pidgin: AI get long dey discuss as a threat to jobs and livelihoods. But wetin’s di reality? For dis new series, we explore di impact e na don already having on different occupations – and how pipul true true feel about dem AI assistants.

paragraphs.english: These are not small numbers. And adoption is moving fast. A US survey found that nearly four in ten workers were using generative AI at work by mid-2025. This pace of adoption outstrips the early years of both the personal computer and the internet. Across countries in the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), firms report that AI integration into business functions is accelerating.
paragraphs.pidgin: Dis ones dey no small numbers. And adoption dey move fast. A US survey find tok nearly four for ten workers bin dey using generative AI for work by mid-2025. Dis pace of adoption outstrips di early years of both di personal computer and di internet. Across countries for di Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), firms report dat AI integration into business functions dey accelerate.

paragraphs.english: So the productivity story is real, particularly in text-heavy, codifiable tasks across legal, finance, marketing and customer service. That much is not hype.
paragraphs.pidgin: So di productivity story na real, particularly for text-heavy, codifiable tasks across legal, finance, marketing and customer service. Dat plenti na no hype.

paragraphs.english: But the apocalyptic predictions have not yet materialised. Employment across OECD countries remains historically robust. A review of the research-based evidence produced in the US in early 2026 found that despite rapid adoption, AI has so far caused little in the way of widespread job losses or pay cuts. And a study (as yet unpublished) that tracked AI chatbot use in Danish workplaces found essentially zero effects on earnings or recorded hours, even among heavy users and early adopters.
paragraphs.pidgin: But di apocalyptic predictions get no yet materialised. Employment across OECD countries remains historically robust. A review of di research-based evidence produced for di US for early 2026 find tok despite rapid adoption, AI get so far caused small small for di way of widespread job losses or pay cuts. And a study (as yet unpublished) dat tracked AI chatbot use for Danish workplaces find essentially zero effects on earnings or recorded hours, even among heavy users and early adopters.

paragraphs.english: Why? Because many jobs still require tacit knowledge, physical presence, sound judgement and the kind of contextual awareness that AI cannot yet replicate. And adoption is far more uneven than the headline numbers suggest. While AI use among firms in the US soared between 2023 and 2025, a report found fewer firms had actually embedded it into their operations. The information sector, for example, adopted it at roughly ten times the rate of hospitality.
paragraphs.pidgin: Why? Because plenti jobs still require tacit knowledge, physical presence, sound judgement and di kind of contextual awareness dat AI no fit yet replicate. And adoption na far more uneven than di headline numbers suggest. While AI use among firms for di US soared between 2023 and 2025, a report find fewer firms bin get actually embedded e into dem operations. Di information sector, for example, adopted e for roughly ten times di rate of hospitality.

paragraphs.english: One economic modelling exercise estimates that AI might add somewhere between 1% and 1.6% to US GDP over the next decade. This is significant, but it is far short of the transformative claims.
paragraphs.pidgin: One economic modelling exercise estimates dat AI might add somewhere between 1% and 1.6% to US GDP over di next decade. dis na big, but e na far short of di transformative claims.

paragraphs.english: The gap between productivity gains in controlled studies and real transformation inside organisations remains enormous. The revolution, for most workplaces, has not yet arrived.
paragraphs.pidgin: Di gap between productivity gains for controlled studies and real transformation inside organisations remains enormous. Di revolution, for most workplaces, get no yet arrived.

paragraphs.english: Here is where the story gets more consequential and where the commentary falls short. The distributional effects of AI within developed economies deserve far more attention. Not everyone is experiencing this transformation the same way.
paragraphs.pidgin: Here na where di story gets more consequential and where di commentary falls short. Di distributional effects of AI inside develop economies deserve far more attention. No everybody dey experience dis transformation di same way.

paragraphs.english: The evidence on who benefits is strikingly consistent. Less experienced workers see the biggest gains from AI tools. A study found that AI narrowed the gap between the most and least productive staff, with the largest improvements among lower-ability workers.
paragraphs.pidgin: Di evidence on who benefits na strikingly consistent. Less experienced workers see di biggest gains from AI tools. A study find tok AI narrowed di gap between di most and least productive staff, wit di largest improvements among lower-ability workers.

paragraphs.english: In customer service, novice agents benefited most. The most experienced staff experienced little improvement and, in some cases, slight quality declines. The industry experiment mentioned above found below-average performers improved by 43%, while top performers gained 17%. So the biggest gains go to the least experienced workers, narrowing the gap between top and bottom performers within firms.
paragraphs.pidgin: For customer service, novice agents benefited most. Di most experienced staff experienced small small improvement and, for some cases, slight quality declines. Di industry experiment mentioned above find below-average performers improve by 43%, while top performers gained 17%. So di biggest gains go to di least experienced workers, narrowing di gap between top and bottom performers inside firms.

paragraphs.english: That sounds like good news. But there’s a catch.
paragraphs.pidgin: Dat sounds like good news. But there’s a catch.

paragraphs.english: While AI may compress skills inside firms, the broader labour market is telling a different story. Entry-level roles are shrinking in AI-exposed occupations. The routine tasks that once justified hiring juniors – jobs which provided learning opportunities for those on the bottom rung – are the first to be automated.
paragraphs.pidgin: While AI may compress skills inside firms, di broader labour market dey tel a different story. Entry-level roles dey shrinke for AI-exposed occupations. Di routine tasks dat once justified hiring juniors – jobs which provided learning opportunities for dem ones on di bottom rung – dey di first to be automated.

paragraphs.english: Economic theory has long warned that automation displaces workers from tasks, and the creation of new tasks to counterbalance this is neither automatic nor guaranteed. An estimated 60% of jobs in advanced economies face some AI exposure.
paragraphs.pidgin: Economic theory get long warn tok automation displaces workers from tasks, and di creation of new tasks to counterbalance dis na neither automatic nor guaranteed. An estimated 60% of jobs for advanced economies face some AI exposure.

paragraphs.english: In most realistic scenarios, inequality worsens without deliberate intervention – partly because higher-income workers hold more capital assets and stand to gain from rising returns on AI-related investments.
paragraphs.pidgin: For most realistic scenarios, inequality worsens without deliberate intervention – partly because higher-income workers hold more capital assets and stand to gain from rising returns on AI-related investments.

paragraphs.english: The pattern that is emerging is this: AI helps those already inside the door while quietly narrowing the door for those trying to get in.
paragraphs.pidgin: Di pattern dat dey emerge na dis: AI helps dem ones don already inside di door while quietly narrowing di door for dem ones trying to get for.

paragraphs.english: Sector matters. Firm size matters. Job type matters. The AI transition is not one story. It is many – unfolding at different speeds, with different consequences, depending on where you sit in the economy.
paragraphs.pidgin: Sector matters. Firm size matters. Job type matters. Di AI transition na no one story. e na plenti – unfolding for different speeds, wit different consequences, depending on where you sit for di economy.

paragraphs.english: The debate has been stuck between breathless optimism and existential dread. Neither is useful. The evidence points somewhere more uncomfortable: a transformation that is real but partial, fast in some corners and stalled in others – and distributing its costs and benefits in ways that are shaped by existing inequalities.
paragraphs.pidgin: Di debate don dey stuck between breathless optimism and existential dread. Neither na useful. Di evidence points somewhere more uncomfortable: a transformation dat na real but partial, fast for some corners and stalled for others – and distributing im costs and benefits for ways dat dey shaped by existing inequalities.

paragraphs.english: If the productivity gains are genuine, the question is: who captures them? If entry-level work is disappearing, what replaces it? And if the gap between firms that adopt and those that cannot is widening, the focus should be on what we are building in response. Just talking about it won’t be enough.
paragraphs.pidgin: If di productivity gains dey genuine, di question na: who captures dem? If entry-level work dey disappeare, wetin replaces e? And if di gap between firms dat adopt and dem ones dat no fit dey widene, di focus suppose to be on wetin we dey builde for response. Just talking about e win’t be enough.
